original_abstract: Precious metal coordination complexes with $d^6$ valence electron configurations, such as Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III), serve as the foundation for modern lighting technologies, solar energy conversion, and photocatalysis. However, developing comparable complexes using abundant first-row transition metals has remained a significant challenge. While research in this area has historically centered on Fe(II), we demonstrate that isoelectronic Cr(0) complexes can achieve photoluminescence quantum yields and excited-state lifetimes that far exceed those of any previously reported first-row $d^6$ system. The metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) luminescence of these Cr(0) complexes is highly competitive with benchmark Os(II) polypyridines. Furthermore, we show that these MLCT states can be effectively harnessed for photoredox catalysis, enabling benchmark chemical reductions to proceed efficiently under low-energy red light. Our findings suggest that strategic molecular design can unlock the potential of abundant first-row $d^6$ metals, opening new frontiers in photophysics and sustainable chemistry.
humanized_abstract: The development of new precious metal coordination complexes has driven advancements in the fields of lighting, energy production and photocatalysis, among others. Achieving analogous functions using abundant first-row transition metals is therefore highly desirable. To date, most research has focused on the iron(II) ion, here we show that isoelectronic chromium(0) complexes lead to luminescence with higher photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) and excited-state lifetimes than any known first-row d6 system to date. Additionally, their metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) emission is comparable to benchmarking that of the well-studied and highly active complexes based on the second-row transition metal center, osmium(II) in a polypyridyl system. Their MLCT states can therefore be also efficiently employed for photoredox catalysis. Red light excited photoredox catalysis gives access to benchmark reductions of relevant substrates, confirming that highly efficient low-energy photoactivable catalysts can be obtained using such affordable and abundant transition metal center. Our studies therefore show that complexes with a suitable architecture can be readily and efficiently obtained for the abundant first-row d6 transition metal center and give access to new photophysics and sustainable photoredox chemistry.

Additionally, their metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) emission is comparable to benchmarking that of the well-studied and highly active complexes based on the second-row transition metal center, osmium(II) in a polypyridyl system. Their MLCT states can therefore be also efficiently employed for photoredox catalysis. Red light excited photoredox catalysis gives access to benchmark reductions of relevant substrates, confirming that highly efficient low-energy photoactivable catalysts can be obtained using such affordable and abundant transition metal center. Our studies therefore show that complexes with a suitable architecture can be readily and efficiently obtained for the abundant first-row d6 transition metal center and give access to new photophysics and sustainable photoredox chemistry.
